Employee : Hi , just a heads up, I’ve attached the formal letter via email but I am giving my two weeks. My last day will be the 28th.

Boss : (Silence)

Employee : Hello...

Boss : Morning. Can you send over the report file when you get a sec?

Employee : Sure thing

Boss :  Thanks.

Employee : Did you see my message above?

Boss : Yes I’ll get to it, for now client is asking for the report by 11.

Employee :It's been sent

Boss :  (Silence)

Employee : Can we find 15 minutes today to talk about my resignation.

Boss :  Bit slammed today. Can you jump on the 2pm with the client team?

Employee : I can, but I need to discuss the handover.

Boss : Handover for what?

Employee : My resignation

Boss : (Silence)

Employee : I have booked 4pm in your calendar

Boss : Had to move it, sorry. Can you take the client's call at 4 instead? They asked for you specifically.

Employee : I'll take the call. I still need the 15 minutes

Boss : Noted

Employee : It’s been four days. I’ve CC’d HR directly. My last day is the 28th. That’s confirmed with them.

Boss : But I'm away until the 29th?

Employee : This is why I tried to get a call in, this the third time I’ve told you.

Boss : Let's talk when I'm back.

Manager : Where are you?

Employee : It's -10 degrees, the roads are frozen

Manager : Yes, where are you? We're all here at the office

Employee : You know I don't drive when it's below freezing. I've mentioned this multiple times including during my interview.

Manager : I understand you’re nervous, but we still need you here for the 10:00am client meeting.

Employee : Again I can’t drive in these conditions. I have severe anxiety about winter driving after my accident three years ago. I can work from home and take the call remotely.

Manager :  Is there someone who could drive you in? A family member or roommate?

Employee : No reasonable person is driving in this weather. The local government issued a travel advisory at 6:00 A.M. I’m working from home today and I’ll join the call from here.

Manager : The temperature just went up to -2 degrees. Any chance you can come in now?

Employee : That’s when the roads are most dangerous. Black ice forms when temperatures hover around freezing. No, I’m not risking my life for a meeting that I can attend virtually. Do you actually want me to die on black ice?

Manager : I obviously do not want you to die on black ice

Employee :Then stop asking me to drive in unsafe conditions. I’m logged in and available for the full workday from home.

Manager : Your Teams status has been showing as away for the past 45 minutes. Are you actually working?

Employee: Yes. I’ve been working on the quarterly report. We were literally on a call together at 2:00 PM discussing the exact document I’m working on right now.

Manager : Your status says away. That means you’re not at your computer.

Employee: I’m at my computer. I’m typing this message to you from my computer. Teams sets my status to away when I’m working in other applications for extended periods. I’ve been in Excel for the last hour building the financial models you asked for.

Manager :I find that hard to believe. If you were actually working, your status would be green.

Employee: That’s not how Teams works. The status updates based on mouse movement and activity within Teams specifically, not activity across my entire computer. I sent you three emails in the past hour with updated projections. Check your inbox.

Manager : Anyone can send emails. That doesn’t prove you’re actively working.

Employee:  I was on a video call with you 90 minutes ago. You watched me share my screen. You saw me working on this exact file. I’ve completed 12 of the 15 deliverables you assigned yesterday. What exactly do you think I’m doing?

Manager : Okay, fine. But going forward, you need to make sure your Teams status stays green. I need to be able to see that you’re online and available

Employee: My Teams status will continue to show away when I’m working in other applications. That’s how the software functions. If you need to reach me, send me a message and I’ll respond within 10 minutes like I always do.

Manager : I still expect to see that green dot. Can you just move your mouse every few minutes?

10 Features of a Standout CV 

Want to have 3-5 interviews in 2 months? Here is how to write your CV

1. Clean, Easy-to-Read Layout: First impressions matter. Keep your CV structured, with clear headings and enough white space for easy scanning.

2. Tailored for the Role: One CV doesn’t fit all jobs. Match your skills and experience to the job description to show you're the perfect fit.

3. Powerful Personal Summary: In a few lines, highlight who you are, what you bring, and why you’re the best candidate. Hook them from the start!

4. Achievements Over Duties: Don’t just list tasks — show impact. Use action verbs and numbers to highlight your results.

5. ATS-Friendly Keywords: Use relevant keywords from the job description to get past Applicant Tracking Systems.

6. Updated Contact Details: Make sure your phone, email, and LinkedIn profile are accurate and easy to find.

7. Relevant Skills Section: Highlight both technical and soft skills that align with the job requirements.

8. Clear Work Experience: List jobs in reverse order, showcasing contributions and achievements in each role.

9. Education & Certifications: Include degrees, relevant courses, and certifications that add value to your profile.

10. Polished and Error-Free: Typos can cost you opportunities. Proofread carefully or get someone to review it for you.

Manager: Are you planning to come today?

Employee : No. I broke my leg yesterday. I’m in a cast and on crutches. I sent you a photo of the X-ray and the doctors note last night at 9pm.

Manager:  I saw that, how long will you be out? 

Employee : The doctor said minimum four weeks before I can put weight on it. I’ll be working remotely starting tomorrow once the pain medication wears off enough for me to focus.

Manager: We really need you here for the team standup meetings. Can you make it in just for those?

Employee : I can’t drive. My right leg is broken and I have a manual transmission. The standup meetings are 15 minutes long. I can join those on video.

Manager: What about taking the Uber?

Employee : I’m not taking an Uber to the office to sit through a 15-minute meeting when I can do my entire job from home. I had surgery yesterday. I’m on prescription painkillers.

Manager: The thing is, we really value in-person collaboration. What if we got you a chair? You could sit during the meetings.

Employee : I already have a chair. At my house, where I’ll be working for the next four weeks as stated in the medical documentation I provided.

Manager: Not showing up won't be good for you.

Employee : I’m not coming into the office with a broken leg. This is not negotiable. I will be working remotely as outlined in my doctor’s note and as permitted under the company’s medical leave policy. Stop asking.

during a job interview, a gen z was asked why he wanted to join the company, he replied 'is this company even worth joining'

HR: We lost another senior employee today.

CEO: What happened?

HR: He resigned after receiving an external offer.

CEO: That makes no sense. We could have matched it.

HR: That is the issue. We were willing to pay a stranger 70% more for the same role, but would not give our existing employee even a 20% raise.

CEO: External hiring is different. That is market pricing.

HR: He noticed that too.

CEO: We appreciated his loyalty. He had been here for years.

HR: Yes. And during those years, he consistently exceeded expectations while being told to “wait for the next review cycle.”

CEO: But budgets are complicated for internal employees.

HR: Apparently not for external candidates. The new hire budget was approved in three days. His raise request sat for eight months.

CEO: We had to stay competitive in the hiring market.

HR: He was part of that same market. The only difference is that another company valued him before we did.

CEO: So he left over salary?

HR: Not just salary. He left because he realized loyalty was being rewarded less than leaving.

CEO: That is unfortunate.

HR: Yes. Companies will sometimes trust a candidate after a 45-minute interview more than an employee who already proved themselves for five years.

CEO: So what are you saying?

HR: If companies only recognize employee value after a resignation letter appears, then eventually employees will stop waiting to be appreciated internally.

Sometimes the fastest way for an employee to get market value is to stop being your employee.

Relative: So where are you working these days?

Me: A tech company. The pay is great, we get yearly raises of around 30%, full work-from-home, real unlimited PTO, and they actually respect personal time.

Relative: Hmm… I’ve never heard of that company before.

Me: That doesn’t really mean anything. It’s still a great company.

Relative: No offense, but if nobody knows the name, it’s probably a small company.

Me: Small company with better pay, better benefits, and less stress than most famous companies.

Relative: Still… I feel like a “real job” is when one can recognize the company name immediately.

Me: And that mindset is exactly why people stay miserable at big-name companies just for social validation.

For seven years, he gave everything to the company.

He exceeded expectations year after year.
Never missed deadlines.
Never caused problems.
Never received a disciplinary warning.

Management trusted him enough to let him train new hires.
Over time, he trained entire teams.
Some of the employees he mentored were eventually promoted into senior positions.

Whenever systems failed, people called him.
Whenever clients escalated issues, managers relied on him.
Whenever leadership needed stability, he was the dependable employee they pointed to as “an example of company culture.”

Then one day, he resigned.

Professionally.

He submitted the required notice period.
Prepared transition documents.
Agreed to train his replacement.
Spent weeks handing over knowledge to ensure the company would continue operating smoothly after his departure.

And the moment HR received the resignation letter…

Everything changed.

His system access was quietly restricted.
Passwords were reset.
Internal meetings suddenly excluded him.
Managers who praised him for years became cold and distant overnight.

Then security was instructed not to allow him into certain parts of the compound without clearance.

An employee who had spent seven years building trust was suddenly treated like a threat in less than 24 hours.

Confused, he asked why this was happening.

HR simply responded:
"It is now common practice for our business to release employees from their notice before it is up."

That answer stayed in his mind the entire notice period.

If he was trusted enough to train employees…
Trusted enough to access critical systems…
Trusted enough to represent the company for seven years…

Why did all that trust disappear the moment he decided to leave?

That’s the part many employees eventually realize.

In some companies, loyalty only exists for as long as you stay.

The second you choose yourself, the company starts treating you like you already belong to someone else.
Fun fact: The company suddenly finds budget the moment you resign.
The employee who trains everyone else is often paid less than the people they trained.
Companies love “open communication” until employees communicate openly.

A company proudly announced a new “People First” initiative.

The CEO posted a long message on social media about empathy, employee wellbeing, and building a healthy workplace culture.

HR rolled out new posters across the office:

“Your mental health matters.”
“Take breaks.”
“Family comes first.”

Employees were even encouraged to attend a mandatory wellness webinar called:
“Preventing Burnout in the Modern Workplace.”

Ironically, the webinar was scheduled for 7PM after working hours.

One employee joined the session from home while finishing a spreadsheet that had been marked “URGENT” fifteen minutes earlier.

Halfway through the webinar, the speaker said:

“Remember, no job is worth sacrificing your personal life.”

At that exact moment, messages started flooding the company Teams chat.

Manager:
“Need everyone online tonight.”

Another manager:
“Client escalated issue. Nobody logs off until this is resolved.”

Someone else:
“Please prioritize this before tomorrow morning.”

The employee stared at the webinar on one screen and the flood of late-night work messages on the other.

That’s when it clicked.

In many companies, corporate wellness exists the same way office decorations do.

It’s there to look good in photos.
Not necessarily to be used.

A new employee joined the company and immediately noticed something strange about corporate culture.

Every senior manager constantly talked about “burnout,” “toxic work habits,” and the dangers of overworking.

Town halls were filled with speeches about work-life balance.
HR sent monthly wellness newsletters.
Leadership kept warning employees about the mental health impact of stress.
But the employee also noticed something else.
The same managers sending wellness emails were online at 2AM.
The same executives talking about balance praised people for “going above and beyond” on weekends.
The same company discouraging burnout quietly rewarded the employees who never logged off.

Curious, the employee questioned one of the senior directors.

“If overworking is so unhealthy,” he asked, “how do you all know so much about it?”
The director laughed awkwardly and changed the subject.
A few weeks later, the employee received an invitation to an “exclusive leadership strategy session.”

It wasn’t in a conference room.
It was at a luxury hotel bar.

That night, senior leaders sat around casually discussing the very habits they publicly condemned.

One executive bragged about surviving on four hours of sleep.
Another laughed about answering emails during his honeymoon.
Someone proudly admitted they hadn’t taken a real vacation in six years.

The employee finally understood.

Corporate culture works a lot like that.
The people warning employees about burnout are often the same people normalizing it behind closed doors.
Why do unemployed friends they eventually get a job?

HR: Are you comfortable working under pressure?

Gen Z: I can handle pressure, but I believe salary should match the workload and expectations of the role. Fair compensation helps people perform at their best.

HR: Of course. We just want people who can thrive in a fast-paced environment.

Gen Z: “Fast-paced environment” is usually corporate language for “multiple jobs without multiple salaries.”

HR: No, we just value flexibility and adaptability here.

Gen Z: Flexibility for who though? Because companies love flexibility when employees stay late, answer messages at night, and cover extra work. Suddenly flexibility disappears when employees ask for better pay or more time off.

HR: We expect team players who are willing to go the extra mile.

Gen Z: I don’t mind going the extra mile. I just think the extra mile should come with extra compensation instead of being rebranded as “passion” during interviews.

Stop Using One CV for Every Application! But what if you’re applying for the same role every time? Do you still need a new CV for every application?

One of the biggest mistakes job seekers make is using the same CV for every job. Employers can tell when your CV is generic, and it reduces your chances of standing out.

But what if you’re applying for the same role every time? Do you still need a new CV for every application?

Not necessarily!
Here’s What You Should Do Instead:

✅ Optimize for Keywords – Different companies use different terminology. Match your CV’s wording with the job description.

✅ Highlight Relevant Achievements – Tweak your bullet points to showcase the most relevant experience and achievements according to the job description.

✅ Customize Your Summary/Profile Section – A quick way to tailor your CV is by adjusting your summary/profile section to reflect the company’s values, keywords, or priorities.

✅ Top CV Mistakes to Avoid (And What to Do Instead)

1. Unprofessional Email Address
Use a clean, professional email—ideally your name (e.g., firstname.lastname@gmail.com). Avoid nicknames or outdated domains like @yahoo or @hotmail.

2. Personal Details (Age, Marital Status, Religion, Full Address)
Not only are they irrelevant—they can lead to unconscious bias. Stick to your name, phone, email, LinkedIn, and city/state if needed.

3. Objective Statements
Outdated and vague. Replace with a compelling Professional Summary that highlights your unique value, achievements, and goals.

4. Irrelevant Experience or Skills
Tailor your CV to the role. Remove experience or skills that don't add value or distract from your core strengths.

5. "References Available Upon Request"
Recruiters assume this. Use the space to highlight your wins, certifications, or impact instead.

6. Generic Buzzwords Without Evidence
"Hardworking" or "team player" means little without proof. Show results instead: “Led a team of 5 to launch a product in 3 months.”

7. Long Paragraphs
Recruiters skim. Use bullet points and action verbs to improve readability and impact.

8. Photos (unless required)
In many regions, including a photo is unnecessary and could invite bias. Skip it unless you're in a region or industry (e.g., modeling, acting) that requires it.

9. Hobbies & Interests
Only include if they showcase leadership, creativity, or align with the role (e.g., coding side projects, volunteering, public speaking).

ATS-COMPLIANT CV

When a CV is ATS compliant, it means it is structured and formatted in a way that can be easily read, scanned, and understood by an Applicant Tracking System (ATS).

What is ATS ?

An ATS is software many companies use to screen CVs before a human recruiter even sees them. The system parses your CV to check for keywords, skills, experience, and qualifications that match the job description.

Now for a CV to be ATS compliant, it should:

✅ Use a simple format – No complicated tables, graphics, or unusual fonts that the ATS can’t read.

✅ Include keywords – Mirror the skills and keywords from the job description (e.g., “Talent Acquisition,” “Data Analysis,” “Project Management”), depending on the job ad.

✅ Use standard headings – Like Work Experience, Education, Skills instead of creative alternatives that the ATS might miss.

✅ Save in the right file type – Usually .docx or PDF (if the employer accepts PDF).

✅ Avoid images or text boxes – ATS often can’t read text inside them.

✅ Be achievement-focused – Highlight measurable results, not just duties.

📌 In short:

An ATS-compliant CV is clean, keyword-optimized, and recruiter-friendly. It ensures your CV doesn’t get filtered out by the system before it reaches human eyes.

✅ CV Self-Audit Checklist - At what point should you review/revamp your CV?

1. Interview Response Check

- Am I getting interview invites from my applications?

- If not, is it because of my CV (not ATS-friendly, not tailored, not achievement-focused)?

2. Content Relevance Check

- Does my CV reflect my current career goals and target role?

- Is it tailored to the industry I want to work in?

- Have I highlighted transferable skills (if I’m changing fields)?

3. Updates Check

- Have I added my most recent job, promotion, or role change?

- Did I include new certifications, courses, or technical skills?

- Are my achievements measurable (e.g., “increased sales by 25%”) and not just duties?

4. Structure & Format Check

- Is my CV ATS-compliant (simple structure, correct keywords, no complex graphics)?

- Does it fit into 2 pages (for most professionals) and 1 page (for entry level)?

- Is the layout modern, easy to read, and professional-looking?

5. Career Growth Check

- Does my CV show progression (growth in roles, skills, and achievements)?

- Is it strong enough to position me for the next level (senior, manager, executive)?

6. Regular Review Check

- Have I reviewed/updated my CV in the last 6–12 months?

- If a recruiter called me today, would I feel confident sharing my CV as it is?

👉 If you check “No” on 2 or more of these, it’s time to review or revamp your CV.

Bro offered to help because the boss had been stressed all day, and even at 20, he could tell he was drowning a bit.

Pretty diligent for a 20-year-old, really.

Could’ve been cruising around in his Cortina with the boys instead. Maybe headed to some entertainment joint.

So bro handed him something — probably a can of paint — and said:
“There you go mate.”

Just automatic. It’s a common phrase across the country.

The boss slammed the can on the counter. Face twisted with rage.

Then hissed: “I’M NOT YOUR F**KEN MATE. I’M YOUR BOSS.”

Bro felt like an ant.

He cried right there in front of him.

Bro wasn’t a small bloke either — six foot, solid enough — but in that moment, he felt like a little kid again.

Over 30 years later, he still thinks about those five words.

Not with bitterness anymore. He’s actually come full circle on it.

But the lesson burned itself permanently into his brain:

YOU ARE NOT BETTER THAN ANYONE.
ACT LIKE A HUMAN BEING.

People never forget how you make them feel.

Job Hunting Tips

1. Nail Your Resume Format
Your resume is everything. In just 1-2 pages, you need to give a snapshot of who you are, what you’ve done, and what skills you bring to the table.

Updating your resume regularly and tailoring it to each role is crucial. Don’t just use the same one you had for internships. Take the time to re-read it, refine it, and make sure it speaks to the recruiter who has no idea who you are. This is the first step in getting noticed—so make it count!

2. Make It ATS-Friendly (Applicant Tracking System)
Nowadays, most companies use AI to filter resumes before a human even sees them.

Make sure resume is ATS-friendly by including the right keywords.

It’s important to tweak your resume for each position and include keywords that match the job description. It’s a little extra effort, but it can make a huge difference in getting past that first stage.

3. Get a Second Pair of Eyes (or a Few!)

This means that you need to start seeking for feedback.

Reach out to your supervisor, manager, department head, and HR to review your resume.

Their input will give you a fresh perspective and help improve your resume.

But make sure it reflects and talks about you. after all, you know yourself best

4. Be Consistent and Start Early
Applying for jobs is a numbers game.

Set a target of your daily job applications - i.e. 5-10 applications a day

Don’t expect immediate results; patience is key. The earlier you start, the better!

5. Be Patient, but Don’t Lose Momentum

Job hunting doesn’t happen overnight.

There are days when you won't hear back from anyone, and it’s easy to feel discouraged.

But keep reminding yourself to stay active and keep tweaking your resume, learning a new skill, or networking. Keep your momentum going, and eventually, something will click.

To everyone in the middle of their job search—stay focused, keep going, and don’t give up. The right opportunity is out there. Have faith, all the best and good luck!

Things to remove from your CV

1. Career Objective: Avoid starting your CV with a generic career objective that focuses on what you seek rather than highlighting your value to the role. You can include an objective if you are doing a career pivot or if you are a fresh graduate.

2. Personal Information: Omit details like home address, ID number, dependents, health info, date of birth, marital status, and religion. Adding this information on a CV may pose a concern for discrimination during the recruitment process.

3. Photo: Unless appearance is crucial for the job like the hospitality industry, exclude photos to prevent bias and keep the focus on your skills and experience. Some countries may require photos so you need to also do your research.

4. Additional Documents: Don't combine certificates, ID copies, etc., with your CV. Keep your CV as a standalone file. Combining the documents may disadvantage you especially in this era of using bots during initial screening.

5. Buzzwords: Steer clear of overused terms like "hard-working" and "motivated" to avoid blending in with other candidates. Instead include action verbs that tallies with the impact you want to bring out on your CV.

6. Graphics: This may make your CV clumsy or too beautiful but lacking proper value based content. Also some ATS systems may struggle with graphics, so skip logos, charts, and tables that could hinder initial scanning.

7. Unnecessary Fluff: Remove cover pages, titles like "Curriculum Vitae of...", and anything that doesn't contribute to a concise, impactful CV. Remember a Recruiter has 6 six to skim through your CV in order to make a decision so you need to make it short and straight to the point.

8. Long Paragraphs: Break information into bullet points for readability. Keep introductory summaries and responsibilities brief. Also, don’t make the bullets too long.

9. Life Story: Your CV isn't a Wikipedia page. Focus on a clear structure and a succinct Unique Value Proposition, resembling a website's landing page.

10. Work Email Address: Using your current employer's email is unprofessional. Opt for a personal email to maintain a polished image.

Is Your CV ATS-Friendly? Here’s Why It Matters

In today’s competitive job market, your CV isn’t just reviewed by humans—it’s often screened by Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) first. These systems filter resumes based on keywords and formatting, so even if you’re the perfect candidate, a poorly formatted CV could hold you back.

Here’s how to make your resume ATS-friendly:

✅ Use a simple, clean format: Avoid graphics, tables, and complex layouts that ATS might not read properly.
✅ Incorporate relevant keywords: Tailor your CV to match the job description. Highlight skills, qualifications, and experience using the exact terms the employer uses.
✅ Stick to standard fonts: Use fonts like Arial, Calibri, or Times New Roman to ensure readability.
✅ Use proper headings: Label sections clearly (e.g., “Work Experience,” “Education,” “Skills”).
✅ Save it in the right format: Submit your CV as a Word document (.doc or .docx) unless the job specifies otherwise.

Remember, your goal is to impress the ATS and the recruiter who will see your resume after it passes the first screen.

7 Do's & Don'ts of a Resume:

Every detail of your ATS resume sends a message to the interviewer. Whether it's the layout, color scheme, or the presentation of your responsibilities and accomplishments, each element carries significant importance.

[1] Naming of files
- While sharing your resume, name the file properly.
- It eliminates the chances of your resume getting lost in the pile.
- Use the format [Name] - [Last Name] - Resume

[2] Format
- Make your resume reader-friendly.
- Add white space.
- Use bullet points.

[3] Email address
- Never share an unprofessional email address.
- If you don’t have a proper email address, create a new one for your professional journey.
- Caromsupa@gmail.com, partyanimal@gmail.com are not professional email addresses
- Use this format [Name] - [Last Name] - [Number] @gmail.com

[4] Colors, Bolds & Italics
- Don't use too many colors. It makes the resume difficult to read.
- Stick to 1-2 colors.
- Bold the sub-headlines & italicize to highlight a specific achievement, if any.

[5] Cliches and Jargons
- Avoid the overused and cliche words.
- Instead, use action words and results to demonstrate your skills.

[6] Spelling & Grammatical errors
- No matter how careful you’ve been, there are chances of minor errors. Such errors indicate that you lack attention to detail.
- Proofread your resume at least three times.

[7] Numbers
- Quantify your results whenever possible. It creates a more powerful impact on the recruiter.

Five days earlier, he had submitted his two weeks’ notice. He did everything “the right way.” Gave them time. Stayed professional.

Then on Monday, HR called him.

“It is now common practice for our business to release employees before their notice period is complete.”

And just like that, he lost a week of pay he was counting on.

His manager? Never said a word. No goodbye. No “thanks for your time here.”
Nothing.

We’re told our entire careers to give two weeks’ notice. Be professional. Don’t burn bridges. Leave on good terms.

But when it’s their turn?
They can cut you loose whenever it’s convenient and call it “common practice.”

The courtesy only goes one direction.

He’s not bitter. He’s just awake now.

If you’re still in corporate, protect yourself.

Don’t assume they’ll honor anything.
And if you’ve been thinking about building something of your own?

Maybe this is your sign.

How to craft an ATS CV

Creating an Applicant Tracking System (ATS) friendly CV is crucial because many companies use these systems to screen resumes before they ever reach human eyes.

Here's how to optimize your CV for ATS:

1. Use Standard Formatting:
-Headers: Use clear headers like "Work Experience," "Education," "Skills."
-Fonts: Stick to professional fonts like Arial, Calibri, or Times New Roman. Avoid overly stylized fonts.
-Bullet Points: Use simple bullet points for listing items, not fancy symbols.

2. Tailor Your CV with Keywords:
-Job Description: Mirror the language from the job posting. If the job description mentions "project management," ensure your CV includes this term if applicable.
-Hard Skills: Include specific skills like "Python," "SEO,"  etc., especially if they're mentioned in the job ad.

3. Simple Layout:
- Avoid: Graphics, images, charts, or tables as ATS might not read them correctly.
-Columns: Use a single-column format. Multiple columns can confuse ATS.

4. File Type:
- Submit in .pdf file format
- pdf keeps your formatting intact.
- Check the application instructions

5. Contact Information:
- Simple and Clear: Just your name, phone number, email, and perhaps LinkedIn profile or personal website if relevant.
- No need for address unless requested.

6. Work Experience:
- Format:
Job Title
Company Name | City, County
Month Year - Month Year
- Description using action verbs like "Managed," "Developed," "Led."
- Quantify Achievements: Use numbers where possible, e.g., "Increased sales by 20% over one year."
7. Education:

-Standard Format:
Degree Name
University Name, Location
Graduation Year
8. Skills Section:

- List Relevant Skills: Only include skills that are relevant to the job.
- Group them if necessary (e.g., Technical Skills, Soft Skills).
9. Avoid:

- Headers and Footers: Sometimes ATS can miss information here.
- Abbreviations: Spell out terms at least once (e.g., "Search Engine Optimization (SEO)") before using abbreviations.
10. Spelling and Grammar:

- Check: Even though ATS focuses on keywords, spelling errors can still cause issues or might be noticed if your resume passes to a human reviewer.
11. Customize Each Application:

- Don't Use a One-Size-Fits-All: Tailor your CV for each job application.
- This might mean slight adjustments in how you present your experience or which skills you highlight.
12. Test Your Resume:

- ATS Simulators: There are online tools where you can upload your resume to see how an ATS might read it.
- This can give you an idea of what might need adjusting.
13. Remove the following

-Marital status, Date of Birth, Religion, ID Number
-Irrelevant Work experience, skills and hobbies
-Your high school and primary details if you have attended college
- Inappropriate email addresses (e.g. CaroMsupa@gmail.com)
-Irrelevant stuff on your CV long might in most cases work against you.
Remember, while optimizing for ATS, your CV should still be readable and appealing to human eyes because, ultimately, it's humans who make the hiring decision. Balancing ATS optimization with a clear, professional presentation is key.

Characteristics of a Good Resume.

1- Use only Cambria, Calibri or Helvetica fonts.
This is important, you know. Many jobseekers submit resumes full of strange fonts. HR also gets dizzy reading what you write in your resume. So avoid it, okay?

2- The margins of the 4 corners of the resume must be 1 inch.
Basically, this is to make the resume look neat. HR will be satisfied and it won't hurt their eyes because it's neat and organized.

3- Clear headings. You can make the font a little bigger or use capital letters.
Apart from the type of font, sizing is also important. If there is something you want to highlight in your resume, then make the font a little bigger, so HR can and will easily distinguish it.

4- Don't be too dense. Make sure there is space.
This is a mistake that many people make. Did you know that HR only takes a few minutes to filter resumes. So if the resume is very congested, they will just reject it because they don't have time to read it.

5- One page is enough.
If you want to send a resume to apply for a job, make sure the resume is only one or two pages. Don't send a resume that is up to 5 pages, there are many cases where HR won't even read the entire resume.

A resume that is pleasing to the eye can make HR read the resume straight away.

Your CV is your first impression—make it count!

Are you looking to land that dream job? Here are some quick tips to ensure your resume stands out:

1. Keep it concise
- One page if you have less than five years of experience.
- Two pages if you're aiming for a senior position.

2. Include essential details
- Always include your name, email, LinkedIn ID, and phone number at the top.
- Avoid outdated info like your address or a photo. ❌

3. Ditch the career objective
- Instead of a generic career objective, use a professional summary.
- Example: “Results-oriented marketing professional with 5+ years of experience in developing and executing successful marketing campaigns. Proven ability to drive traffic, generate leads, and increase sales with expertise in digital marketing, social media marketing, and email marketing.”

4. Use the PAR framework
- When describing your work experience, focus on the Problem you faced, the Action you took, and the Result you achieved.
- Example: “Led a team to develop a new content strategy (Problem) by introducing data-driven insights and A/B testing (Action), resulting in a 30% increase in website traffic in six months (Result).”

5. Follow the XYZ structure for achievements
- Clearly state your accomplishment (X), the result (Y), and what you did to achieve it (Z).
- Example: “Increased sales by 15% (Y) by developing and implementing a new marketing campaign (Z).”

Remember, your CV is a powerful tool—use it wisely to make a strong first impression!

Things to Remove from your CV

1. Career Objective: Avoid starting your CV with a generic career objective that focuses on what you seek rather than highlighting your value to the role. You can include an objective if you are doing a career pivot or if you are a fresh graduate.

2. Personal Information: Omit details like home address, ID number, dependents, health info, date of birth, marital status, and religion. Adding this information on a CV may pose a concern for discrimination during the recruitment process.

3. Photo: Unless appearance is crucial for the job like the hospitality industry, exclude photos to prevent bias and keep the focus on your skills and experience. Some countries may require photos so you need to also do your research.

4. Additional Documents: Don't combine certificates, ID copies, etc., with your CV. Keep your CV as a standalone file. Combining the documents may disadvantage you especially in this era of using bots during initial screening.

5. Buzzwords: Steer clear of overused terms like "hard-working" and "motivated" to avoid blending in with other candidates. Instead include action verbs that tallies with the impact you want to bring out on your CV.

6. Graphics: This may make your CV clumsy or too beautiful but lacking proper value based content. Also some ATS systems may struggle with graphics, so skip logos, charts, and tables that could hinder initial scanning.

7. Unnecessary Fluff: Remove cover pages, titles like "Curriculum Vitae of...", and anything that doesn't contribute to a concise, impactful CV. Remember a Recruiter has 6 six to skim through your CV in order to make a decision so you need to make it short and straight to the point.

8. Long Paragraphs: Break information into bullet points for readability. Keep introductory summaries and responsibilities brief. Also, don’t make the bullets too long.

9. Life Story: Your CV isn't a Wikipedia page. Focus on a clear structure and a succinct Unique Value Proposition, resembling a website's landing page.

10. Work Email Address: Using your current employer's email is unprofessional. Opt for a personal email to maintain a polished image.

Top CV Mistakes to Avoid (And What to Do Instead)

1. Unprofessional Email Address

3. Objective Statements
Outdated and vague. Replace with a compelling Professional Summary that highlights your unique value, achievements, and goals.

4. Irrelevant Experience or Skills
Tailor your CV to the role. Remove experience or skills that don't add value or distract from your core strengths.

5. "References Available Upon Request"
Recruiters assume this. Use the space to highlight your wins, certifications, or impact instead.

6. Generic Buzzwords Without Evidence
"Hardworking" or "team player" means little without proof. Show results instead: “Led a team of 5 to launch a product in 3 months.”

7. Long Paragraphs
Recruiters skim. Use bullet points and action verbs to improve readability and impact.

8. Photos (unless required)
In many regions, including a photo is unnecessary and could invite bias. Skip it unless you're in a region or industry (e.g., modeling, acting) that requires it.

9. Hobbies & Interests
Only include if they showcase leadership, creativity, or align with the role (e.g., coding side projects, volunteering, public speaking).

Resume Content That Gets You Hired

1. CONTACT INFORMATION
- Full Name
- Professional Email (avoid casual ones)
- Phone Number
- Portfolio/Website Link (Optional)

2. PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY (ABOUT YOU)
A short, powerful intro about who you are and what you bring.

Example:
Creative UX Designer with 3+ years of experience crafting user-centered websites and apps.

3. PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY (SKILLS-BASED)
Highlight skills that match the job description.

-  Technical Skills: e.g., HTML, Figma, Data Analysis
-  Soft Skills: e.g., Teamwork, Problem-Solving

4. WORK EXPERIENCE
-  Job Title | Company | Dates
-  2–4 bullet points explaining:
i. What you did
ii. How you added value
-  Quantify results (e.g., “Increased website traffic by 30%”)
5. EDUCATION
-  Degree | University | Year

- Relevant Coursework (Optional)
-  Academic Results (if strong)
6. PROJECTS
Highlight skills that match the job description.
-  Technical Skills: e.g., HTML, Figma, Data Analysis
-  Soft Skills: e.g., Teamwork, Problem-Solving

7. CONTENT FORMULA FOR EVERY WORK EXPERIENCE (STAR METHOD)
-  Situation: Brief background
-  Task: What was your role?
-  Action: What did you do?

-  Result: What did you achieve? (Preferably with numbers)
8. SKILLS TO HIGHLIGHT (BASED ON TRENDS)
i. Hard Skills
-  Data Analysis
-  SEO/SEM

-  Project Management Tools
-  Coding/Automation
-  AI/ML Basics
ii. Soft Skills
-  Problem-Solving
-  Critical Thinking
-  Communication

-  Adaptability
-  Creativity
9. COMMON RESUME MISTAKES TO AVOID
- Typos and grammatical errors
-  Using one generic resume for all jobs
-  Stuffing buzzwords without context

-  Using overly complicated templates
-  Including outdated skills (e.g., MS Office Basics)
-  Adding full home address (city and country are enough)
10. IDEAL RESUME LAYOUT RULES
-  Margin: 0.5” – 1”
-  Font: Professional (Calibri, Helvetica, Arial)
-  Font Size: 10–12 pt

-  Line Spacing: 1.0–1.15
-  Length:
i. 1 page (less than 10 years experience)
ii. 2 pages max (senior roles)
11. ADVANCED PRO TIPS TO STAND OUT
-  Use Keywords Smartly: Sprinkle them naturally throughout your resume
-  Show Growth: Highlight promotions and evolving responsibilities
-  Customize Your Title: Tailor your resume file name (e.g., First name_Second name_UXDesigner_Resume.pdf)

- Portfolio/Case Studies: Add a clickable link if in creative/technical fields
- Metrics > Responsibilities: Focus on achievements, not just duties
12. ATS Optimization Tips
- Use standard headings like “Work Experience” and “Skills”
- Prioritize recent experience
- Use white space for readability

- Include relevant links (E.g portfolio, GitHub)

How to Make Your CV ATS-Compliant

An ATS (Applicant Tracking System) doesn’t read your CV like a human.
It scans, ranks, and filters before a recruiter ever sees it.
If your CV isn’t ATS-friendly, it may never be read, no matter how qualified you are.

1. Use a Simple, Clean Format
ATS struggles with design.

- Avoid tables, text boxes, icons, and graphics
Use a single-column layout
- Stick to standard fonts (Calibri, Arial, Times New Roman, Georgia)
- Use clear headings
Simple beats fancy. Always.

2. Use the Right Keywords
ATS scans for keywords from the job description.
- Skills
- Job titles
- Tools
- Certifications

👉 Match the language in the job description naturally in your CV.

3. Use Standard Section Headings
ATS understands common headings better.
Use:
- Professional Summary
- Work Experience
- Skills
- Education
- Certifications

Avoid creative headings like “What I Bring to the Table”.

4. Write Achievements, Not Duties
ATS and recruiters both score impact. Instead of:
“Responsible for managing projects”
Use:
“Managed 5+ projects, delivering 100% on time and within budget”
Numbers improve ranking and readability.

5. Save in the Right Format
-Best options: PDF (text-based) or .docx
-Avoid image-based PDFs

If the text can’t be highlighted, ATS can’t read it.

6. Avoid Headers, Footers & Symbols
ATS may ignore:
-Information in headers/footers
-Symbols, emojis, and special characters

Keep important details in the main body.

7. Tailor Your CV for Every Role
One CV for 50 jobs rarely works.
-Adjust keywords
-Reorder skills
-Align your summary with the role

Tailoring increases ATS score dramatically.

👉Final Truth
ATS compliance doesn’t guarantee a job,
but non-compliance almost guarantees rejection.
Your CV must first pass the machine
before it can impress the human.

10 Features of a Standout CV

Want to have 3-5 interviews in 2 months? Here is how to write your CV

1. Clean, Easy-to-Read Layout: First impressions matter. Keep your CV structured, with clear headings and enough white space for easy scanning.

2. Tailored for the Role: One CV doesn’t fit all jobs. Match your skills and experience to the job description to show you're the perfect fit.

3. Powerful Personal Summary: In a few lines, highlight who you are, what you bring, and why you’re the best candidate. Hook them from the start!

4. Achievements Over Duties: Don’t just list tasks — show impact. Use action verbs and numbers to highlight your results.

5. ATS-Friendly Keywords: Use relevant keywords from the job description to get past Applicant Tracking Systems.

6. Updated Contact Details: Make sure your phone, email, and LinkedIn profile are accurate and easy to find.

7. Relevant Skills Section: Highlight both technical and soft skills that align with the job requirements.

8. Clear Work Experience: List jobs in reverse order, showcasing contributions and achievements in each role.

9. Education & Certifications: Include degrees, relevant courses, and certifications that add value to your profile.

10. Polished and Error-Free: Typos can cost you opportunities. Proofread carefully or get someone to review it for you.
